label: Chapter Nine
title: The Handwriting of Justice
question: What does trust become when oath, war, and beauty hold the same thread?
heroAlt: Jem, Marok, and Kitsu facing a dark mirror written in Kitsu's golden legal hand
summary: Kitsu's stolen oath-thread is reclaimed, Marok and Justice finally name the wound between them, and Jem refuses to be anyone's prize.
hook: Which half do they steal first: the Menagerie below, or the courthouse across the river?
body: The mirror wore Kitsu’s handwriting like a stolen face. Not ink. Not paint. Gold script burned across the dark glass in the exact legal hand I had watched him use in court: precise, merciless, so neat it made elegance look undisciplined.
NO FOXES. NO JUDGMENT.
The words rang without sound. Behind them, somewhere inside the mirror, half of the Scale sang in a low golden note that slipped beneath my ribs and worried the empty place behind my spine.

Kitsu did not move. Marok did. One step back. Only one. But in the Menagerie chamber, surrounded by shattered mirrors and smoke still green from his restored tail, that step was louder than accusation.
Kitsu heard it. Of course he did. His face remained calm, which meant nothing good had survived underneath. “That is my hand,” he said.
Marok’s restored tail flicked once behind him, bright green and furious. “Yes. We noticed.”
“I did not write it.”
“No one accused you.”
“You stepped away.”
Marok’s jaw tightened. “That was my body being sensible before my mouth could stop it.”
“An impressive novelty,” I said.
Neither of them smiled. Unkind.

The room held its breath around us. Black mirrors lay broken across the floor like a hundred dead witnesses. Every shard reflected the gold script differently. In one, the warning was backwards. In one, it was written across Kitsu’s throat. In another, his hand held the quill. I looked away from that one. Too late. The Menagerie had noticed me noticing.
The wall behind the mirror rippled. A woman’s laugh passed through the velvet, sweet as sugared rot. The hostess had not gone far.
“Justice signs beautifully,” she said from nowhere and everywhere. “Even when he does not remember doing it.”
Kitsu’s gold light snapped under his skin. “Show yourself,” he said.
“No,” the hostess replied. “I prefer being wanted.”
“No one wants you,” I said.
A pause. “Beauty lies prettily when cornered.”
I smiled at the mirror because my temper had found its posture. “Beauty bites when bored.”
The velvet walls shivered. Marok glanced at me as if he approved and worried in the same breath. An exhausting talent.

Kitsu stepped toward the mirror. Gold light gathered around his right hand, smelling of cold parchment and struck bells. The script answered. Each letter sharpened until the room filled with a sound like quills scraping bone.
“Do not touch it,” I said.
Kitsu stopped. Good. I disliked how quickly that pleased me.
“Why?” he asked.
I stepped over a shard of mirror. My borrowed feet objected. I ignored them. Feet had been overrepresented in my concerns since dawn and deserved less power.
The gold writing looked perfect. Naturally. Perfect things are where rot loves to hide.
I lifted my hand, stopped before my fingers reached the glass, and let Beauty sight open.

The room soured. Honey, smoke, and regret curdled into wet iron. The velvet darkened. The gold script softened at the edges, not melting, exactly. Bruising. Beneath the beautiful legal hand, something silver had been stitched through the letters.
Thread. No. Not thread. Oath-thread. Kitsu’s.
I lowered my hand. “Someone used you,” I said.
Kitsu went stiller than before. Marok’s face changed too. Less suspicion. More horror. That suited him badly.
“Explain,” Kitsu said.
I pointed at the writing. “The hand is yours. The will is not. Your oath-thread is laced beneath the words. Like a signature cut from skin and sewn onto a lie.”
Marok swore softly. Kitsu did not. That was worse.
“Impossible,” he said.
“Tonight has made that word cheap.”
His mouth tightened. “If my oath-thread was used, then something reached my tail.”
“Your tail is still missing,” Marok said.
“I am aware.”
“And now your hand is on the warning I received.”
“I am aware of that as well.”
“Were you aware before?”
The question landed between them. Hard. Ugly. Necessary.
Kitsu’s eyes cut to Marok. Gold, bright and dangerous. “No.”
“Truth?” Marok asked.
Kitsu’s expression became frighteningly polite. “Would you like me to bind myself and prove it?”
“I would like one answer tonight that does not arrive after everything breaks.”
“Then perhaps you should have given us the warning before the Scale did.”
Green fire flashed. Gold answered. The chamber warmed and chilled at once. I stepped between them before War and Justice could turn grief into architecture.
“Enough.”
They looked at me. Good.

My voice came sharper than I intended. “Marok hid a warning. Kitsu’s oath was stolen. I am missing a tail, wearing a coat that is not mine, and standing in a pleasure house where the furniture has opinions. If either of you needs a duel, schedule it after we survive.”
Marok looked away first. Kitsu did not. Of course.
“The writing is a lock,” I said. “And a lure. It wants Kitsu to touch it.”
“Because it is keyed to me,” Kitsu said.
“Because it wants whatever is left of you.”
His face did not move. “Useful distinction.”
“I thought so.”

Behind the mirror, the Scale half sang again. The note slipped through the floor and into every shard. The broken mirrors turned toward Kitsu like flowers seeking sunlight. In each one, he appeared different. Kitsu beside the God of Justice, gold tail immaculate. Kitsu alone in a hall of laws, both hands bloodless and clenched. Kitsu watching me dance with Marok. Kitsu kissing me in rainwater. Kitsu turning away too quickly afterward, as if desire were something he could apologize back into its cage.
My mouth went dry. Marok saw the reflections too. Of course he did. His restored tail lowered.
“Kitsu,” he said, quieter now.
Kitsu’s gaze remained fixed on the mirror. “Do not.”
“I was not—“
“Do not be kind because the room has become cruel.”
Marok’s face tightened. “That is not the only time I can manage it.”
Kitsu laughed once. No humor. Just a blade finding stone. “Forgive me. I had not noticed your excellent consistency.”

The words struck Marok. He absorbed them without the usual flourish. That made it hurt more. I hated both of them for making me care about the shape of their wounds while my own spine felt unfinished.
“Look at me,” I said.
Kitsu did. Stupid of him. Braver than he knew.

His control was there, but thin as ice over a river that had already decided to drown a city. The mirror had found his private want and dressed it in legal script, because of course a place like this knew the cruelest way to undress Justice was to make him appear guilty.
“I believe you,” I said.
The words surprised him. They surprised me more.
Marok looked at me, then at him. “So do I.”
Kitsu’s throat moved. “You stepped away,” he said.
“Yes,” Marok replied. “And then I came back.”
Truth. No gold wire needed.
The mirror cracked wider. Silver light leaked from the wound in the glass. The hostess laughed again, farther away now. Annoyed. Excellent.
“How do we open it?” Marok asked.
Kitsu looked at the gold script. “I must reclaim the oath-thread.”
“That sounds painful,” I said.
“Likely.”
“That was not concern. That was accusation.”
“Then accurately received.”
Marok stepped beside him. “What do you need?”
Kitsu glanced at him. Not distrust. Not forgiveness. Something unfinished.
“If I pull the thread free, the silver bind will try to write through me again.”
“Then I burn it,” Marok said.
“Only the silver.”
“I know.”
“Do you?”
Marok’s green eyes flashed. Then he breathed out. “Yes. I know.”
Kitsu looked at me. “And you reveal the lie.”
“Always.”
“No.” His voice softened. “Not always. Only if you choose.”
That was inconvenient. Worse, it was learned.
I stepped toward the mirror. “Then I choose.”
Kitsu lifted his hand toward the writing. Stopped. Looked at me. “May I use your sight as anchor?”
Marok went very still.

I understood the question beneath the question. Kitsu could not kiss me again. Not here. Not with Marok standing beside us still carrying the shape of my mouth on his own. Not with the Menagerie watching like a spider in velvet. But he needed an anchor. Want, restrained until it learned manners.
I held out my hand, palm inward. A choice he could step into or refuse. “You may hold my hand,” I said.
His fingers closed around mine. Careful. Too careful.
Then Marok’s hand appeared beside us, palm up, green fire hovering beneath his skin. “For the burn,” he said.
Kitsu looked at it. For one terrible heartbeat, I thought he would refuse. Then Justice took War’s hand.

The room changed. Rose sparked through my arm. Green flared in Marok’s palm. Gold tightened around Kitsu’s fingers. The braided thread snapped into being between us, brighter than before and painfully alive.
The mirror shrieked.
“Hold,” Kitsu said.
“I hate that word,” I said.
“I know.”
“Good.”
He touched the writing. Gold light exploded.
Kitsu’s body locked. His hand pressed flat to the mirror as the legal script raced up his wrist, over his forearm, toward his throat. Letters appeared beneath his skin.
NO FOXES.
NO JUDGMENT.

Marok swore and drove green fire into the silver thread. The room filled with the scent of rain striking hot stone, cold parchment, and extinguished candles. My sight opened so violently that every beautiful surface peeled back at once. The velvet became old rot. The mirrors became mouths. The gold writing became a hook lodged in Kitsu’s oath.
There. Rot beneath law.
“Now,” I gasped.

Marok burned. Not wildly. Not too much. He burned exactly what I showed him, green fire cutting the silver without touching gold. Kitsu shook between us. His grip tightened around my hand, then loosened at once. Always catching himself. Always caging himself before anyone else could.
“Do not disappear into it,” I said.
His eyes found mine through the light. “I am here.”
“Then stay.”

The gold script tore free of the mirror and snapped back into his palm. Kitsu made no sound. That was worse. His knees buckled. Marok caught him. Not by accident. Not as a joke. He stepped in and held him upright with one arm around his ribs, green fire still trembling in his other hand.
Kitsu stared at him. Marok stared back. “No debt,” Marok said, voice rough.
Kitsu’s expression broke for half a second. Not into softness. Into pain.

The mirror shattered outward without touching us. Behind it, half of the Scale of Judgment hung suspended in black air. One broken pan. Half a beam. Gold and blackened gold braided through with rose, green, and silver scars. It sang like a verdict underwater.
I reached for it. The Scale half withdrew. Rude.
A second note answered from far away. East. Higher. Colder. Law-scented and blood-bright. Kitsu’s head snapped toward it. “The other half,” he said.

The first half spun once in the air. In its broken surface, I saw two places at once. The Menagerie below us, roots of mirror and velvet wrapped around one half. A courthouse across the river, black gates and gold seals, where Kitsu’s missing tail burned like a captured sunrise.
Then, for one breath, my own magenta tail appeared behind both images. Not in a cage. In a crown.
I stepped back. Marok noticed. Kitsu noticed. Naturally.
“Two halves,” Marok said. “Two places.”
“A narrowing window,” Kitsu added.
“And only three of us,” I said.
The Scale half vanished into the dark behind the wall, still singing. Not gone. Waiting. The Menagerie exhaled around us. The danger did not pass. It merely became scheduled.
Marok released Kitsu as soon as Kitsu could stand. Kitsu straightened, perfect and pale and furious about needing anyone. Marok’s hand fell to his side as if it had never held him.

The lie between them remained. Not the warning. The other one. The one where Marok pretended not to care whether Kitsu trusted him. The one where Kitsu pretended distrust was safer than wanting anything from War. The one where both of them looked at me because it was easier than looking at each other.
I had tolerated many things tonight. This was not going on the list.
“Both of you,” I said.
They turned. “No.” I pointed between them. “At each other.”
Marok blinked. “Jem—“
“Do not Jem me in that tone. I have been dragged through mirrors, markets, false law, two kisses, and at least three emotional revelations I did not order. I am tired.”
Kitsu’s mouth tightened. “We do not have time for this.”
“That sentence is what cowards say when they are afraid honesty will take longer than survival.”
He went still. Good.
Marok looked at the broken mirror floor. “Kitsu thinks I caused this.”
“I think you contributed,” Kitsu said.
“There he is.”
“You did.”
“I know.”
The words were bare. Kitsu’s expression shifted.

Marok swallowed. “I know I broke the Scale. I know I hid the warning. I know I tried to be clever and made myself useful to a trap. I know.” His voice cracked. He hated that. I saw him hate it. “But I did not want you severed. Either of you.”
Kitsu looked at him for a long moment. Then he said, “I know.”
Marok laughed once. Broken. “That is all?”
“No,” Kitsu said. “It is all I can say without making it worse.”
“Try.”
The word surprised all of us. Especially Marok.
Kitsu’s hands curled at his sides. “When you are reckless, I become law because someone must survive the consequence.”
“And when you are law,” Marok said, “I become reckless because someone must prove there is a world outside your cage.”
Kitsu flinched. I did too. Not because it was cruel. Because it was accurate.
The room went quiet. I stepped toward them before either could retreat into dignity. “Excellent. Painful and useful. We are learning.”
“Are we?” Marok asked.
“Unfortunately.”
Kitsu looked at me then, and the heat in his eyes was not only from the stolen oath-thread. Marok looked too, green restored and raw. Want moved through the room like something with a pulse.
Desire was a door. Desire was also an argument no one had the courage to finish. I was done being the doorway they stared through.
I took Marok’s hand first. His breath caught. I took Kitsu’s next. His fingers trembled once before closing around mine.
“You both want me,” I said.
Marok’s eyes darkened. Kitsu stopped breathing.
“Do not insult me by pretending otherwise. Do not insult each other by using me as the place you hide from what that means.”
“Jem,” Kitsu said, barely sound.
“Ask,” I said.
Two gazes. Two hungers. Two kinds of fear.
Marok asked first. Of course. “May I?”
Kitsu’s voice followed, rougher than rain. “May I?”

I pulled them both down to me and kissed them before either could become noble about it. Marok tasted of smoke and honey and grief refusing to bow. Kitsu tasted of cold bells and restraint breaking under its own holiness. For one breath, they were not touching each other, only me between them. Then the braided thread flared rose-green-gold so bright the shattered mirrors lit like dawn.
Marok’s hand tightened at my waist and stopped there, waiting. Kitsu’s fingers touched my jaw and did not claim it. I broke the kiss before it could become a choice I had not made carefully enough.
My heart behaved terribly. My mouth worse. Both males looked ruined. Good.
“Figure out who gets me,” I said.
Marok made a sound like prayer and panic. Kitsu’s gold eyes went very still. I released them both.

“And by gets,” I added, because men and gods are poor listeners when desire is standing nearby, “I mean earns the right to be asked again. I am not a relic. I am not a reward. I am not the prettiest excuse either of you has for avoiding the other.”
Marok’s restored tail burned brighter. Kitsu’s stolen oath-thread glowed in his palm, clean now, his own again.
Somewhere behind the walls, one Scale half sang below us. Across the river, the other answered from the courthouse where Justice kept its cages. The Menagerie waited. The Bell breathed.
I wiped my mouth with the back of my hand and smiled because Beauty had taught me many things, including how to make danger look invited. “Now,” I said. “Which half do we steal first?”

label: Chapter Ten
title: Two Halves, One Choice
question: Which hand does Jem take when the Scale forces the night to divide?
heroAlt: Jem standing between Marok and Kitsu as both offer a hand and mirror paths open behind them
summary: The chosen path recovers one Scale half, the other fox returns with the second, and all three realize the next door is beneath Beauty itself.
hook: Below the city, something hidden sings back.
body: The Menagerie waited for my question to become a mistake.
Which half do we steal first? I had said it with all the confidence of a woman whose borrowed knees had not recently considered retirement. The room answered by singing.

Not loudly. That would have been merciful. The half of the Scale hidden behind the velvet wall released one low golden note, then let its twin answer from far across the river. Two sounds. Two graves. Two pieces of divine judgment pretending they had not once been whole.
Kitsu turned toward the distant note. Marok turned toward the near one. I remained between them, which was apparently my new spiritual vocation.

The shattered mirrors around us lifted their broken faces. Each shard reflected a different exit. One led down, under the Menagerie, into a dark root of velvet and want. The other showed rain, black gates, and a courthouse crouched across the river like a verdict waiting to be fed.
Inside the courthouse reflection, gold light burned behind iron bars. Kitsu’s tail. His face did not change. That was how I knew it hurt.
Marok’s restored green tail flared once behind him. Not with triumph. With anger. It suited him better than guilt, which had made him look too human and not nearly annoying enough.
The first Scale half sang beneath our feet again. The Menagerie answered with a shiver of velvet walls. Somewhere in the house, the hostess laughed softly, like a knife being wrapped for gift-giving.
“We are not alone,” Kitsu said.
“We have not been alone since the mirror learned to smile,” I replied.
Marok looked toward the broken wall. “The Menagerie half is close. Below us, I think. Desire has roots here. The house will have wrapped itself around the Scale like a drunk around a secret.”
Kitsu’s gaze remained on the courthouse reflection. “The other half has entered legal ground. If the next Bell rings before it is claimed, it may become part of the false commencement record.”
Naturally, the night had developed paperwork.
“What does that mean?” I asked.
“It means if we choose one together, we may lose the other,” Kitsu said.
Marok went still. “We split.”
“No,” Kitsu and I said together.
For a moment, that almost pleased me. Almost. Then the Scale sang again, and the sound crawled into the unfinished place behind my spine.
Kitsu closed his recovered oath-thread inside his fist. Gold light leaked between his fingers. “He is right.”
I hated the sentence for many reasons, beginning with he and ending with right.
Marok looked at me. Not grinning. Not performing. Worse. Waiting. The kind of waiting that offered me a door and did not push.

The mirror-shards rearranged themselves. Down, the Menagerie. Across, the courthouse. Below, want. Across, law. One route wrapped in velvet. One written in gold. Both shaped like cages that had learned my name.
Of course. The universe had finally found a way to make the choice insulting.
Kitsu spoke first. “Marok knows the Menagerie’s lower paths. I know the courthouse procedures.”
“Procedure is a very generous word for a building that appears to eat fox tails,” Marok said.
“You are welcome to bring your expertise in being eaten by pleasure houses.”
“I survived.”
“Barely.”
“Beautifully.”
I lifted one hand. “If you both flirt through jurisdictional crisis, I will choose the Scale and leave you here.”
Marok blinked. “Was that flirting?”
Kitsu’s mouth tightened. “No.”
Truth, but not enough of it.
The Menagerie trembled. The courthouse reflection brightened. The two Scale notes began to drift apart, one lower, one higher, as if the broken instrument were teaching us how to be divided.
Marok offered his hand, palm up. A choice, not a claim. “Come below with me. I know how this house lies.”
Kitsu offered his hand differently, palm inward. A threshold I could step into or refuse. “Come across with me. I know how the law lies.”
I stared at both hands.
Desire was a door. So was trust. So was stupidity, apparently, and all three had begun wearing attractive faces.
The next Bell breathed above the city. The glass would remember which hand I took.

— CHOICE · GO WITH MAROK —
If I had learned anything tonight, it was that going down rarely improved a situation. Naturally, I took Marok’s hand.

Kitsu’s fingers curled once, then fell to his side. He did not look away. That made leaving with Marok feel less like a victory and more like tearing a page from a book before knowing whether it held the answer.
“Do not die,” I told him.
Kitsu’s eyes moved from my hand in Marok’s to my face. “I had no plans.”
“Make better ones.”
Something almost warm crossed his face. Then he turned toward the courthouse reflection and stepped through it like a man entering his own execution with excellent posture. The mirror swallowed him.
Marok’s hand tightened around mine for exactly one heartbeat, then loosened. “No debt.”
“You are overusing that phrase.”
“I am trying to make it true.”
That was unfair. I followed him through the broken wall before I could say something kind by accident.

Below the Menagerie, velvet became root. The staircase curved downward through red-dark fabric that pulsed softly against the walls. Mirrors hung between folds like wet eyes. In each, I saw some version of want. Mortals kneeling. Spirits bargaining. My goddess smiling. Me standing alone with my magenta tail restored, all eyes on me and no hands at my throat.
Beautiful lie. I kept walking.
Marok’s shoulder brushed mine in the narrowing passage. “Too close?”
“You are large enough to be a structural problem.”
“That was not an answer.”
I looked at him. Even in green shadow, he waited. No grin. No assumption. Only heat trying to behave.
“Not too close,” I said.
His breath changed. Just once. The passage noticed and tightened around us like a gossip leaning in.

The Scale half lay in a chamber of black velvet roots, suspended over a shallow pool of mirror-water. It was one broken pan and half a beam, blackened gold wrapped in silver thread. The pool showed my tail in its cage. Magenta. Curled tight. Waiting.
My body moved toward it before my dignity could object.
Marok caught my sleeve. Not my wrist. The sleeve. He was learning so aggressively it was becoming inconvenient.
“Jem.”
“I see it.”
“It wants you to.”
Yes. It did. The pool showed my tail closer now. Not frightened. Not trapped. Offered. The beautiful kind of bait. The cruelest kind.

I forced my sight past it. Rot beneath ornament. The tail was not mine. Not here. Only a reflection of wanting stitched to the Scale half with silver. Veyr had built a door out of my desire and trusted me to mistake it for an exit.
Annoying. Accurate. Unforgivable.
“The thread is under the reflection,” I said.
Marok stepped beside me. Green fire gathered in his palms. “Tell me where to burn.”
“If I say stop—“
“I stop.”
“If you start dying impressively—“
“You will be cross.”
“I will be furious.”
His smile flickered. “Better.”
I placed my hand over the mirror-water. It was cold as moonlit glass. The silver thread surfaced, thin and bright, wrapped around the Scale half like a promise that had never intended to be kept.
“There,” I said.

War burned. Not wildly. Not for me. Not for applause. Marok’s green fire cut where I showed him and nowhere else. The thread shrieked. The pool filled with images of every bad decision he had ever made and three he had only considered. I admired the variety despite myself.
Then the reflection of my tail opened its eyes. Veyr’s eyes. Pale. Amused.
No foxes, their mouth shaped without sound.
“No,” I said.
Rose-magenta light tore from my hand. The beautiful lie peeled back. Marok’s fire caught the exposed silver and snapped it clean.
The Scale half dropped. Marok caught it with both hands and immediately went pale.
“That seems bad,” he said.
The broken pan burned green against his palms. Not consuming. Measuring. War holding judgment. A terrible idea, which meant it suited the evening perfectly.
I put my hands over his. “May I?”
He looked at me as if the word still surprised him. “Yes.”
Rose light wrapped over green. The burn eased. The Scale half quieted until it hummed instead of screamed.
For one breath, our hands were everywhere and nowhere: his beneath mine, mine steadying his, the heat between us trying very hard to become a confession.
We had the half.
Somewhere across the river, Kitsu’s note cut through the rain, gold and sharp with danger.
Marok and I looked up together.
“He found trouble,” Marok said.
“Of course he did.”
The mirror-water behind us turned black. In it, the courthouse gates opened like a mouth.
— PATH · SHARED RETURN —
However the glass remembered it, the city returned us to the same bridge.
One Scale half came back marked with velvet and green fire. The other came back marked with law and gold grief. The pieces sang toward each other across the wet air, not whole yet. Not even close.

Marok was bleeding again. Kitsu was paler than marble. I was wearing War’s coat, carrying Justice’s warmth in my bones, and missing the tail Veyr had shown me in a cage because apparently the night believed restraint built character.
It built rage, mostly.

The two Scale halves hovered between us. Rose light sparked along their broken edges. Green fire answered. Gold followed. For one breath, the braid wrapped around the pieces and pulled them almost together.
Almost.
Then the halves jerked apart.
A silver mark appeared along both broken seams: a fox tail curled into a question mark. Veyr’s invitation.
Kitsu looked toward the courthouse. Marok looked back at the Menagerie. I looked downriver, where the city’s glamour shimmered over grief I could suddenly smell beneath the rain.
Rot under ornament. A way in.
The next path was not to a half of the Scale. It was to what Beauty hid beneath the city.
I closed my hands around nothing and felt my missing tail answer from far away.
No foxes, Veyr had said. No Judgment.

I smiled because I had been made by Beauty, trained by a cage, and kissed by two disasters with excellent cheekbones. If Veyr thought I would reach for the prettiest trap first, he had not been paying attention.
“We are not chasing the tail,” I said.
Marok blinked. Kitsu went still.
“Not yet,” I added.
The Scale halves sang. Below the city, something hidden sang back.
kitsu: The Menagerie waited for my question to become a mistake.
Which half do we steal first? I had said it with all the confidence of a woman whose borrowed knees had not recently considered retirement. The room answered by singing.

Not loudly. That would have been merciful. The half of the Scale hidden behind the velvet wall released one low golden note, then let its twin answer from far across the river. Two sounds. Two graves. Two pieces of divine judgment pretending they had not once been whole.
Kitsu turned toward the distant note. Marok turned toward the near one. I remained between them, which was apparently my new spiritual vocation.

The shattered mirrors around us lifted their broken faces. Each shard reflected a different exit. One led down, under the Menagerie, into a dark root of velvet and want. The other showed rain, black gates, and a courthouse crouched across the river like a verdict waiting to be fed.
Inside the courthouse reflection, gold light burned behind iron bars. Kitsu’s tail. His face did not change. That was how I knew it hurt.
Marok’s restored green tail flared once behind him. Not with triumph. With anger. It suited him better than guilt, which had made him look too human and not nearly annoying enough.
The first Scale half sang beneath our feet again. The Menagerie answered with a shiver of velvet walls. Somewhere in the house, the hostess laughed softly, like a knife being wrapped for gift-giving.
“We are not alone,” Kitsu said.
“We have not been alone since the mirror learned to smile,” I replied.
Marok looked toward the broken wall. “The Menagerie half is close. Below us, I think. Desire has roots here. The house will have wrapped itself around the Scale like a drunk around a secret.”
Kitsu’s gaze remained on the courthouse reflection. “The other half has entered legal ground. If the next Bell rings before it is claimed, it may become part of the false commencement record.”
Naturally, the night had developed paperwork.
“What does that mean?” I asked.
“It means if we choose one together, we may lose the other,” Kitsu said.
Marok went still. “We split.”
“No,” Kitsu and I said together.
For a moment, that almost pleased me. Almost. Then the Scale sang again, and the sound crawled into the unfinished place behind my spine.
Kitsu closed his recovered oath-thread inside his fist. Gold light leaked between his fingers. “He is right.”
I hated the sentence for many reasons, beginning with he and ending with right.
Marok looked at me. Not grinning. Not performing. Worse. Waiting. The kind of waiting that offered me a door and did not push.

The mirror-shards rearranged themselves. Down, the Menagerie. Across, the courthouse. Below, want. Across, law. One route wrapped in velvet. One written in gold. Both shaped like cages that had learned my name.
Of course. The universe had finally found a way to make the choice insulting.
Kitsu spoke first. “Marok knows the Menagerie’s lower paths. I know the courthouse procedures.”
“Procedure is a very generous word for a building that appears to eat fox tails,” Marok said.
“You are welcome to bring your expertise in being eaten by pleasure houses.”
“I survived.”
“Barely.”
“Beautifully.”
I lifted one hand. “If you both flirt through jurisdictional crisis, I will choose the Scale and leave you here.”
Marok blinked. “Was that flirting?”
Kitsu’s mouth tightened. “No.”
Truth, but not enough of it.
The Menagerie trembled. The courthouse reflection brightened. The two Scale notes began to drift apart, one lower, one higher, as if the broken instrument were teaching us how to be divided.
Marok offered his hand, palm up. A choice, not a claim. “Come below with me. I know how this house lies.”
Kitsu offered his hand differently, palm inward. A threshold I could step into or refuse. “Come across with me. I know how the law lies.”
I stared at both hands.
Desire was a door. So was trust. So was stupidity, apparently, and all three had begun wearing attractive faces.
The next Bell breathed above the city. The glass would remember which hand I took.
— CHOICE · GO WITH KITSU —
I took Kitsu’s hand.
Marok’s face did not fall. That would have been too easy to forgive. Instead, his smile appeared for half a second and behaved itself badly.
“Do not let the courthouse flirt with you,” he said.
“I will file a complaint if it tries.”
His restored tail flicked once. Green, bright, too alive for how tired he looked. “No debt,” he said.
Kitsu looked at him. “Do not die.”
Marok’s grin softened into something almost serious. “Try to sound less heartbroken about it.”
“Marok.”
“I know. Wrong moment.”
Then he turned toward the lower Menagerie passage and vanished into velvet before either of us could make the moment worse by improving it.
The courthouse reflection swallowed Kitsu and me whole.
Rain struck first. Then cold. Then law.

We stood before black gates across the river, the courthouse rising beyond them in layers of gold seals and dark glass. Every window reflected Kitsu. Not me. Not the street. Kitsu beside the God of Justice. Kitsu without his tail. Kitsu with his hand on a document I could not read. Kitsu kneeling in a room where every law had teeth.
He did not look at any of them. Naturally, I did.
“Your building is rude,” I said.
“It is not my building.”
“It is looking at you like you left it at an altar.”
A muscle moved in his jaw. “Do not bargain with anything beyond the gates.”
“Do I look like someone inclined to bargain with architecture?”
“You look like someone inclined to insult it until it opens.”
The gates unlocked.
I smiled. Kitsu did not, but his silence developed texture.

Inside, the courthouse smelled of old rain, cold parchment, struck bells, and something uglier underneath. Spoiled roses. Beauty magic dressed in legal robes. Of course Veyr had learned to accessorize.
Gold script floated above the central hall.
NO FOXES. NO JUDGMENT.
This time the words were not in Kitsu’s hand. They were trying to become it.
His fingers tightened around mine. Not hard. Enough that I knew he had forgotten he was touching me. Enough that, a heartbeat later, he remembered and loosened at once.
“Stay,” I said.
He looked at me.
“I did not tell you to let go.”
His face became unreadable in the way a locked chest is unreadable while containing a live coal. “Understood.”
We crossed the hall together.

At the center, half of the Scale hung above a judge’s bench carved from black glass. Beside it, behind a lattice of gold procedure, Kitsu’s tail burned like captured sunrise. Straight. Beautiful. Furious. It kept trying to reach him and being corrected by the law around it.
Kitsu stopped breathing.
I hated that tail for hurting him by existing just out of reach.
“The Scale first,” he said.
“That was not what your face said.”
“My face is not the authority.”
“Tragic. It makes several convincing arguments.”
He ignored that with what I suspected was effort.
The gold lattice shifted. Three seals appeared: CLAIM, CONFESS, WITNESS.
“Procedure,” Kitsu said, because apparently the courthouse wished to seduce him specifically.
“Trap,” I said, because I had eyes.
“Both.”
At least we agreed.

I opened Beauty sight. The seals changed. CLAIM glittered brightest, a lovely thing with rot around the edges. CONFESS bled silver. WITNESS looked plain. Almost dull. Which meant it was probably the only honest door in the room.
“Witness,” I said.
Kitsu glanced at me. “The weakest seal.”
“The least pretty one.”
“That is not legal reasoning.”
“It is better.”
For one impossible second, he almost smiled.
He placed his recovered oath-thread against WITNESS. The seal opened. Silver wire lashed out from CLAIM and wrapped around his wrist.
Kitsu did not cry out. Of course he did not. His restraint remained committed to being irritating.
I grabbed the wire with both hands. It burned cold. Beneath it, I saw the lie: Veyr did not need Kitsu to sign the false commencement. He needed Kitsu to believe he already had.
“It is guilt,” I said. “Not law.”
Kitsu’s eyes snapped to mine.
“You think if you had reported every suspicion, obeyed every procedure, anticipated every rot beneath every beautiful thing, none of this would have happened.”
The wire tightened.
“Jem.”
“No. Hear it. This is not your signature. This is your punishment dressed as duty.”
Gold light flared so bright my teeth hurt.
Kitsu lifted his free hand. Stopped. Looked at me. “May I use your sight?”
“You are already holding my hand, Golden One.”
“May I lean on it?”
That was much worse.
“Yes,” I said.
He did. Not with his body. With his trust. Gold law passed through my sight, clean and terrible. I revealed the silver guilt-thread. He cut it with one precise motion.
The lattice opened. The Scale half dropped into his palm.
His tail surged toward him. Kitsu took one step. The courthouse screamed.
The gold lattice slammed down between him and the tail. Every window darkened. False procedure sealed around the tail again, tighter than before.
“No,” I said.
Kitsu’s hand closed around the Scale half until gold light spilled between his fingers. His expression remained calm. Ruined, but calm.
“Not yet,” he said.
“Kitsu.”
“If I break the lattice now, the false commencement takes jurisdiction. We need the Court House proper.”
That sounded like law. It smelled like grief.
I wanted to argue. I wanted to tear the lattice apart with my teeth. Instead, I stepped beside him and looked at his tail burning behind the bars.
“We come back,” I said.
He did not look at me. “Yes.”
“Not as procedure.”
His throat moved.
“As promise,” I said.
He looked then.
The Scale half hummed between us, gold and blackened gold, warming to my rose light and his recovered oath. Across the river, green fire answered from somewhere beneath the Menagerie.
Marok had found his half.
Kitsu and I stood in a courthouse full of beautiful law and left his tail behind.
Not surrendered. Not forgotten. Witnessed.
The gates opened behind us. Rain waited like absolution with poor manners.
— PATH · SHARED RETURN —
However the glass remembered it, the city returned us to the same bridge.
One Scale half came back marked with velvet and green fire. The other came back marked with law and gold grief. The pieces sang toward each other across the wet air, not whole yet. Not even close.

Marok was bleeding again. Kitsu was paler than marble. I was wearing War’s coat, carrying Justice’s warmth in my bones, and missing the tail Veyr had shown me in a cage because apparently the night believed restraint built character.
It built rage, mostly.

The two Scale halves hovered between us. Rose light sparked along their broken edges. Green fire answered. Gold followed. For one breath, the braid wrapped around the pieces and pulled them almost together.
Almost.
Then the halves jerked apart.
A silver mark appeared along both broken seams: a fox tail curled into a question mark. Veyr’s invitation.

Marok blinked. Kitsu went still.
“Not yet,” I added.
The Scale halves sang. Below the city, something hidden sang back.

So I did the insulting thing. The honest thing. I took both hands.
Marok inhaled. Kitsu went still. The Scale made a sound so offended it nearly became language.
“That is not one route,” Kitsu said.
“No,” I replied. “It is mine.”
Marok’s thumb moved once against my knuckles and stopped there, waiting. “Jem.”

label: Chapter Eleven
title: What Beauty Hides
question: What does Beauty reveal when the city has dressed grief in flowers?
heroAlt: Jem, Marok, and Kitsu stand on a rain-lashed bridge beneath two broken Scale halves
summary: The broken Scale leads Jem, Kitsu, and Marok beneath the city's beautiful lie, where Glasswater's drowned prayers reveal the hidden Court House below.
hook: Ahead, the Court House opens its mouth.
body: The Scale halves followed us like two angry moons.

They did not touch. They did not rejoin. They hovered between Kitsu and Marok, one marked with velvet and green fire, the other with law and gold grief, both blackened along the broken seam where Veyr had left his silver question. Rain struck their edges and turned to steam before it reached the bridge stones.
I disliked them immediately.

Not because they were dangerous. Many things were dangerous. Marok had made it his personality. Kitsu had made it a vocation. I disliked the Scale halves because they behaved like beautiful things with unfinished business, and I had spent too long being one of those.
Below the bridge, the hidden song answered again.

Not loud. Not music. A pressure beneath the river, beneath the street, beneath every polished lie the city wore for weather. The note slipped into the unfinished place behind my spine and tugged once.
My missing tail answered from far away. Rude.
Marok turned toward the river. His restored green tail burned low behind him, no longer furious enough to pretend he was not tired. “That is not coming from the Menagerie.”
Kitsu stood beside him, pale as marble under rain and pretending that leaving his tail behind the courthouse lattice had not carved anything important out of him. “Nor the courthouse.”
“Then where?” I asked.
The Scale halves sang again. Beneath us, the river answered with grief.
Kitsu looked down at the water. “Under the city.”
Naturally. The night had run out of respectable directions and was now choosing beneath.

Marok glanced at me. Not at my mouth, which was progress. Not at Kitsu’s hand, which was restraint. At my face, as if he had finally learned that I hated being read poorly more than being read at all. “You said we were not chasing your tail.”
“Not yet.”
“That still sounds like chasing something unpleasant.”
“Everything tonight has been unpleasant. Some of it has had excellent cheekbones.”
Kitsu’s gaze flicked to me, then away too quickly.
Good. No. Bad. Complicated. I was no longer accepting new categories.

The bridge opened onto a broad street lined with flower stalls and gold lamps. Mortals moved beneath striped awnings, laughing with wet hair and red cheeks. A woman sold sugared plums under a canopy of silk. A boy chased a paper fox through puddles. The city had wrapped itself in charm as if charm were a coat and not a confession.
A beautiful lie.
The scent under it was wrong.

Crushed flowers after rain. Honey, smoke, and regret. My magic, but not mine. Beneath that, Veyr’s silver trail: extinguished candles, cheap violets, and wet iron. Beneath that, something older. River silt. Old wood. Prayer drowned before it could reach the ceiling.
My stomach tightened.
Marok noticed. Of course. “Jem?”
“I smell grief.”
He looked at the flower stalls. “I smell plums.”
“That is because grief hired a decorator.”

Kitsu stepped closer to the nearest stall. Buckets of white lilies stood in perfect rows, each bloom beaded with rain. Lovely, at first glance. Hideous upon second sight. The petals had no bruises. No brown edges. No rot. Too perfect for a mortal street where everything else had learned mud.
I opened Beauty sight.
The lilies blackened.

Not dead. Named. Each petal unfolded into a face, then a hand, then a scrap of drowned cloth. The street beneath the flower stalls changed, its gold-lit stones becoming a riverbed. Water rushed where mortals walked. Mouths opened beneath the surface. I heard no screams. That was the worst part. The city had not hidden the screaming. It had removed it.
My knees threatened betrayal. Again. They had formed a union.
Kitsu’s hand hovered near my elbow. “May I?”
I should have said no because I could stand on my own, theoretically, and because being cared for competently had become a dangerous habit. Instead, I looked at his hand and said, “Yes.”

He touched the edge of Marok’s coat at my sleeve. Not my skin. A choice I could step away from. Gold warmth steadied the cloth, then me beneath it. Cold parchment and struck bells threaded through crushed flowers and river grief.
Marok did not touch me. He only stepped to my other side, close enough that War’s heat answered if I wanted it. That restraint was also rude.
The Beauty sight widened. The flower street vanished. For one breath, I stood in Glasswater.

Not the moral. Not the court story. The city. Its streets tilted toward a swollen river under a sky the color of pewter. Mortals crowded rooftops with lanterns and children and hands lifted toward heaven. A shrine bell rang below water. Petitions floated like pale fish. On the highest bridge, a silver-tailed fox stood beside the God of Mercy, who was screaming soundlessly at a sky full of closed doors.
Then the vision folded back into rain, flowers, and plums.
I gasped. Marok said something vicious under his breath. Kitsu did not speak at all.

I hated that I understood Veyr more now than I had one street ago. Understanding was not forgiveness. It was worse. It was a blade placed carefully in your hand with no instruction about where to point it.
“The city is wearing Glasswater,” I said.
Kitsu’s face went still. “Impossible. Glasswater was—“
“Drowned,” Marok said softly.
“Buried,” I corrected. “Under flowers. Under lamps. Under a district pretty enough that no one asks what it cost.”

The Scale halves hummed between us. Rose light crawled along the broken edges. Green answered from Marok’s tail. Gold from Kitsu’s closed fist. For one breath, the braid tried to form around the pieces and failed.
Failed things were becoming crowded tonight.
A puddle beside the curb silvered.
Veyr appeared in it, not fully. Never fully. Silver tail. Pale eyes. A smile like a door opening under water.
No foxes, their mouth shaped.
I stepped closer to the puddle before either male could stop me. “You already said that. I found it unpersuasive.”
The reflected smile widened.
The puddle rippled. Gold letters formed across the water in Kitsu’s hand, then blurred into mine, then into Marok’s, as if Veyr could not decide which wound to use first.
LET THE GODS WITNESS THEMSELVES.

The words smelled of extinguished candles and cheap violets. The mortals around us walked past without seeing them. A man bought lilies. A girl laughed. A woman tucked a white flower into her lover’s coat. Glasswater died under their feet and sold bouquets above it.
My temper found its posture.
“No,” I said.
The puddle went still.
Marok’s voice came low behind me. “No to Veyr, or no to the city?”
“Yes.”
Kitsu looked at the flower stalls, then the street beyond. “If this is a concealed wound, it may hide an entrance.”
“To what?” Marok asked.
“The Court House proper.”

The phrase landed badly. Court House. Not courthouse. Not the building across the river that had locked Kitsu’s tail behind procedure and called grief law. The Court House was older. Deeper. The place divine verdicts passed through before they became pretty enough for gods to announce.
My borrowed ribs tightened. “You knew there was a proper one and let us spend all night in improper architecture?”
“Most assistants never see it,” Kitsu said.
“How comforting. I love being promoted by catastrophe.”
Marok looked down the flower street. “Can we reach it?”
Kitsu’s eyes moved to the Scale halves. “Only through witness.”
“That is your answer to everything.”
“It is often the correct one.”
“Often is doing heroic work there.”

I turned to them both. Rain slipped down my neck. Marok’s coat hung heavy around my shoulders. Kitsu’s warmth had begun to fade from the sleeve. My tail was still missing. His tail was still caged. Marok’s was restored and burning like a reminder that recovery did not mean innocence. The Scale halves hovered between us, incomplete and judgmental.
I was tired.
Not in the body. The body had been complaining for chapters and no longer deserved the spotlight. I was tired in the place Beauty had trained to smile when cornered.
“I need both of you,” I said.
Marok’s expression changed first. Of course. “For what?”
“To hold the sight open. Beauty can reveal the wound, but I do not think Beauty alone can bear it.”
Kitsu looked at me too sharply. “It may hurt you.”
“Many things may. Most have shown initiative.”
“Jem.”
I lifted both hands, palm inward. A choice. Two choices. “May I use you as anchors?”
The word use made Marok flinch. Good. He should hate it. I did too.
“Not like that,” I said, softer. Annoyingly softer. “I am asking. You can refuse.”
Marok stepped into my left hand. “Yes.”
Kitsu stepped into my right. “Yes.”
Their fingers closed around mine.
No one pulled. No one claimed. We held.

Rose light opened in my chest. Green fire answered from Marok, rain on hot stone and smoke. Gold law followed from Kitsu, cold parchment and struck bells. The braided thread snapped into being between us—rose, green, gold—thin as breath and stubborn as a vow no god had approved.
The city glamour screamed.
Mortals did not hear it. They kept buying flowers. Kept laughing. Kept walking over a beautiful lie.
I saw the cost beneath everything.

Every lily became a petition. Every gold lamp became an unanswered prayer. Every mirror in every shopfront showed a drowned street beneath the bright one. Glasswater was not under the city like a corpse. It was threaded through it like guilt. Veyr had not invented the wound. He had found where the gods had dressed it for guests.
My knees buckled. Marok’s hand tightened, then stopped. Waiting. Kitsu’s did the same. I chose to stay standing. Rage helped. Rage remained reliable.
“There,” I said.

A seam had appeared in the street between two flower stalls. Not a crack. A line of blackened gold so thin that only grief could have fit through it. The Scale halves turned toward it and sang in answer.
The nearest mortal vendor looked at me. For one heartbeat, her face changed. Not vendor. Not stranger. A woman on a rooftop holding a child above floodwater. Her mouth formed a word I could not hear.
Then she was only a vendor again, smiling as she arranged lilies over the seam.
A beautiful lie.
“Move the flowers,” I said.

Marok did not ask why. He released my hand only when I nodded, then began lifting buckets away from the seam. Kitsu helped with the careful horror of a man committing petty vandalism in service of divine emergency.
The vendor blinked. “Excuse me—“
I turned Beauty on her.
Not performance. Not polish. Not the soft arrangement my goddess had made of my face for centuries. True Beauty. The kind that reveals what a thing has cost to remain lovely.
The vendor saw the drowned woman beneath herself.
Her smile collapsed.

I caught her before she fell. Or tried. Borrowed bodies remained poorly engineered. Marok caught us both. Kitsu placed one hand over the vendor’s forehead and murmured something that smelled like struck bells.
“She will not remember clearly,” he said.
“Good,” I replied. “Remembering clearly seems awful.”
The seam opened.

No hinge. No handle. Only blackened-gold light spreading between the stones. The flower street bent around it, still pretending. Below, a stairway descended through water that did not wet the steps. The walls were glass. Not clear glass. Memory glass. In every pane, a different mortal prayer pressed its hands to the other side.
The Scale halves lowered toward the opening.
Silver light flashed across the seam.

Veyr’s reflection appeared in the wet stones at our feet. “Now you see it,” they whispered. Their voice sounded like candles drowning. “The pretty part. The part they kept. The part they made you serve.”
Marok’s green tail flared. “Show yourself.”
“Why?” Veyr smiled. “You follow so beautifully without me.”
Kitsu stepped forward, gold light hardening at his fingers. “You used my oath-thread.”
“Justice lent itself to the lie long before I touched it.”
Kitsu’s face did not move. The braid between us trembled anyway.
I squeezed his hand once. He looked at me. Not saved. Not healed. Still wounded. Still here.
“Veyr,” I said.
The silver eyes shifted to me.

I smiled with every cruel lesson my goddess had ever taught me and every better one I had learned tonight. “If you wanted me to see the cost, you should have considered what I do with beautiful lies.”
The reflection narrowed.
“I ruin them,” I said.
Rose-magenta light struck the seam. Green fire followed. Gold law wrapped around both. The silver mark on the Scale halves shrieked, peeled back, and vanished into the rain.
Not defeated. Annoyed. That would do for now.
The hidden stairway opened fully.
At its bottom, far below the flower street and the drowned prayers and the district dressed in lilies, stood blackened-gold gates.

Words were carved across them, but they were too far to read. I did not need to. I felt them scrape the inside of my mouth before I saw them clearly. The Court House did not welcome lies. It sharpened them. It made every falsehood taste like blood before it could leave the tongue.
Marok looked down the stairs. “That seems unfriendly.”
“It is court,” Kitsu said. “It is never friendly.”
“You must have been very popular there.”
“Marok.”
“Wrong moment. I heard it as I said it.”
I laughed once. Small. Terribly timed. Mine.

Both of them looked at me, and for once neither tried to rescue the sound from my mouth. Good. I did not need rescue from laughter. Only from gods, doors, mirrors, missing tails, and occasionally my own knees.

The Scale halves descended first, singing toward the blackened-gold gates. My missing tail answered from somewhere beyond them. Kitsu’s did too. Two notes. Two cages. Two pieces of ourselves waiting inside the place lies went to be punished.
I tightened my hold on both hands.
“Still yes?” I asked.
Marok’s answer came at once. “Yes.”
Kitsu’s was quieter. “Yes.”
The braided thread burned between us, not comfortable, not gentle, not owned.
Chosen.
I stepped onto the first stair.
Behind us, mortals bought lilies under gold lamps and did not remember drowning.
Ahead, the Court House opened its mouth.
And every pretty lie I had ever worn began sharpening its teeth.

label: Chapter Twelve
title: The Court Where Lies Cannot Enter
question: What truth survives in the only court where lies scrape the tongue?
heroAlt: Jem, Marok, and Kitsu descend through a memory-glass stairway with broken Scale halves floating ahead
summary: The Court House forces truth into the open, Kitsu breaks procedure to reclaim his tail, and Jem chooses the bond without letting it become a verdict.
hook: Beyond the door, the undercourt waits with Veyr and Jem's magenta tail.
body: The first stair tasted like blood. Not mine. Not yet.

The Court House announced itself through the tongue before the eye, which felt excessive and therefore exactly like court. One foot on the blackened-gold stair, and copper bloomed along my teeth. The next step scraped something invisible against the roof of my mouth, as if the building had reached in with one polished nail and tested how easily I might lie.
I disliked it immediately.

Below us, the hidden gates waited. Above us, mortals bought lilies over drowned prayers and remembered nothing. The Scale halves floated ahead, one blackened edge flashing green from Marok’s fire, the other gold from Kitsu’s law, both still refusing to touch. They descended through the air with the smug precision of objects that knew more than they intended to explain.
Marok peered down the stairwell. “That seems welcoming.”
“It is not meant to welcome,” Kitsu said.
“Yes, I gathered from the taste of blood.”
Kitsu’s gaze cut to him. “You feel it?”
“I have a mouth, Golden Boy.”
“A frequently unfortunate circumstance.”
Marok’s restored green tail gave one weak flick. “You sound better. I hate that for us.”

I stepped between them before banter could become an excuse for terror. The stairs spiraled down through memory glass. Each pane held a prayer pressed flat against the other side: a mortal hand, a child’s face, a candle flame, a mouth forming words the gods had filed, weighed, and dressed for presentation. The glass did not reflect us. It reflected what we had been made to carry.
Beautiful architecture. Rotten purpose. The taste of blood sharpened with every step.

“No lies,” Kitsu said quietly. His voice had gone formal. Court-formal. The version of him that made rooms lower their eyes. “No evasions if you can avoid them. The Court House weighs statements as they are spoken. Falsehood scrapes the tongue. Perjury takes more.”
“More what?” I asked.
He did not answer quickly enough. Naturally.
Marok looked at him. “More what?”
“Voice,” Kitsu said. “Memory. Sometimes the oath-thread.”
My missing tail throbbed. Rude of it, from a distance.
“So we should tell the truth,” I said.
“Yes.”
“How horrifying.”
The corner of Kitsu’s mouth almost moved. It failed. I forgave it nothing.

At the bottom of the stairs, blackened-gold gates rose from water that did not ripple. They were taller than a god’s vanity and carved with lines of testimony in languages I almost recognized. The words shifted when I looked at them, rearranging themselves into shapes that made my throat ache. Above the join between the gates, an inscription burned in gold.
SPEAK TRUE, OR LET THE TONGUE PAY.
“Subtle,” Marok said.
The gates opened without touching each other. No welcome. No invitation. Only permission sharpened into a doorway.

Inside, the Court House was not a room. It was a wound arranged like one. A long hall stretched beneath the city, black marble veined with gold, its ceiling lost in darkness. Rows of empty witness chairs rose on either side, carved for foxes, gods, mortals, and things older than both. At the far end, a dais held three black scales without pans, each one waiting for a verdict that had not yet learned whom it wanted to ruin.
The air smelled of cold parchment and struck bells. Kitsu’s magic, but older. Less mercy in it.
Marok took one step inside and winced. “I love what they’ve done with the dread.”
The hall answered with a thin gold chime. Marok put a hand to his mouth. “Ow,” he said.
“The Court House dislikes decorative speech,” Kitsu replied.
“Then it and I are unlikely to become intimate.”
Another chime. A brighter wince.
I almost smiled. Almost. Then the Scale halves drifted down the center aisle, and the hall woke around them.

Gold inscriptions kindled along the floor. Lines of law. Petitions. Orders. A river of procedure, all flowing toward the dais. The halves turned toward a side chamber barred in gold lattice. Behind it, something burned like sunrise caught in a net.
Kitsu stopped breathing. His tail.

Gold. Immaculate. Furious in its restraint. It was not caged like Saroka’s tail had been, nor threaded through desire like Marok’s. Kitsu’s tail had been woven through a document suspended in the center of the chamber: a scroll of black parchment written in perfect gold script. His script. The same stolen hand. The same cruel elegance.
Across the parchment, the title burned brighter than the rest.
FALSE COMMENCEMENT AUTHORIZED.
The words scraped my tongue though I had not spoken them.
Kitsu moved toward the lattice. Gold light rose under his skin, then faltered.
Marok did not joke. That frightened me more than the inscription.
“Kitsu,” I said.

He did not answer. His missing tail lashed once through the scroll, and the entire Court House chimed. The false commencement pulsed in response, legal because his witness-thread had been forced through it. Beautiful forgery. Law with a stolen spine.
I opened Beauty sight. The chamber rotted.

The gold lattice became ribs. The scroll became a throat. Kitsu’s tail was threaded through both, not merely validating the forged procedure but feeding it. Each time the Judgment Bell breathed above the city, the scroll drank another thread of his witness and turned the lie closer to truth.
A beautiful lie. A lawful one. I hated it more.
“If we remove the tail,” Kitsu said, voice too calm, “the authorization collapses.”
“Good,” Marok said.
“If we remove it without petition, the Court House may record interference with commencement.”
“Less good,” Marok said.
Kitsu’s eyes did not leave the lattice. “Against me.”
I looked at him. “Because it is your tail.”
“Because it is my hand.”
“Stolen.”
“Still mine.”
The hall chimed softly. Not a punishment. Agreement.
Kitsu’s throat moved. “Procedure requires I file a challenge before the Court House, name the defect, summon a second witness, and await response.”
Marok stared at him. “How long?”
The Judgment Bell answered from far above. Soft. Almost amused.
Kitsu closed his eyes. “Too long.”
“Then we take it,” I said.
He turned toward me. “If I break procedure here, the Court House will remember.”
“Let it.”
“Jem.”

“No.” My voice came sharper than intended. Good. It deserved the edge. “I am tired of rooms that remember law better than suffering. I am tired of pretty lies with proper seals. I am tired of you treating your own pain as acceptable collateral because someone polished the blade before handing it to you.”
The hall went very still.
Kitsu looked at me as if I had put my hand through his ribs and found the truth there beating.
Marok exhaled. “She has a point.”
The Court House chimed.
Marok pressed his lips together. “I am learning to enjoy that sound.”
Kitsu’s mouth tightened. “You should not.”
“Probably not.”
The gold lattice brightened. A new inscription appeared across it, letters forming one by one in severe, perfect script.
PETITION REQUIRED.
Kitsu flinched. Tiny. Terrible. My heart heard it anyway.
He lifted his hand. Gold law gathered around his fingers, careful and exact. “I can file an emergency challenge.”
His voice scraped on can. A bead of blood appeared at the corner of his mouth. I stared at it.
Kitsu wiped it away before either of us could move. “I can—“
More blood.
The Court House chimed again. Not agreement this time. Warning.
“You do not believe that,” I said.
“Belief is not required for procedure.”
The gold floor rang hard enough to hurt my teeth.
Marok stepped forward. “Apparently it is here.”

Kitsu’s control thinned. I watched it happen: the calm tightening, the jaw set, the eyes brightening with a heat he would rather have swallowed whole. His tail burned behind the lattice, feeding a lie one stolen heartbeat at a time.
“If I break this,” he said, “I become the violation.”
I stepped closer to him. Not touching. Not yet. “If you leave it, you become the witness to one.”
That landed. Hard.
Kitsu looked back at the lattice. “May I use you both as anchors?”
Marok answered first. “Yes.”
I held out my hand, palm inward. “Ask properly.”
Kitsu’s gaze cut to me, gold and wounded and too honest for this place. “May I hold your hand while I break the law?”
Ridiculous. Devastating. His.
“Yes,” I said.
He took my hand. Careful. Always careful, even while committing treason against the architecture.
Marok offered his palm to Kitsu next. No joke. No smirk. Only green fire hovering under the skin and something gentler underneath it. “No debt.”
Kitsu looked at the hand for one breath too long. Then Justice took War’s hand.

The braided thread snapped into being—rose, green, gold—bright enough to stain the black marble. The Court House reacted at once. Every empty witness chair turned toward us. The scales on the far dais tilted without pans. The false scroll shuddered.
PETITION REQUIRED.
“No,” Kitsu said. The word did not scrape. It rang.
Gold light flooded his palm. Not the polite law he used to bind vendors or warm coats. This was older. Angrier. Law remembering that procedure was meant to serve truth, not strangle it with ribbons.
The lattice cracked. The Court House screamed in bells.
Kitsu stepped forward, dragging both our hands with him. Not taking. Asking through pressure, waiting for us to follow. I did. Marok did. The braid tightened until my ribs ached.
“Kitsu,” I said.
“I am here.”
“Do not disappear into being correct.”
His mouth twitched once, and somehow that almost undid me. “I will try.”
Then he tore the lattice open. Gold law broke like glass.

The false scroll thrashed. Kitsu’s tail blazed, trapped through its center, and the silver thread around the document lashed toward his throat. Marok burned it before it touched him. Green fire cut through silver. Rose light opened beneath my sight and showed where the lie hid its hinges.
“There,” I said.
Marok burned the hinge. Kitsu pulled his tail free. The Court House struck him.
Not physically. Worse. Every witness chair spoke at once, voices layered and legal and hungry.
UNAUTHORIZED INTERFERENCE. PROCEDURAL VIOLATION. WITNESS COMPROMISED.
Kitsu staggered. His grip crushed my fingers for half a heartbeat, then loosened at once.
“Stop apologizing with your hands,” I snapped.
His eyes found mine through the gold light. “I was not aware I did.”
The Court House chimed. Truth. Infuriating man.

His tail slammed back into him. Kitsu made a sound then. Not a scream. Not quite. A broken breath dragged through clenched teeth, clean and terrible. Gold fire exploded behind him as his tail restored itself, long and bright and shaking with every law he had just betrayed to save it.
The false scroll collapsed to ash. The Scale halves sang.
One blackened half drifted from the center aisle into the opened chamber and lowered itself where the scroll had hung. The other half answered from behind us. For one heartbeat, they almost touched.
Almost.
Then a silver thread snapped between them and pulled them apart again.
The Court House went dark except for the three of us and the restored gold burn of Kitsu’s tail.
Marok still held his hand. Kitsu looked down at their joined fingers as if discovering fire had a shape. Marok released him immediately. Too immediately. I hated that too.
Kitsu swayed. This time, Marok caught him without waiting for collapse. One arm around his ribs. No flourish. No joke. Kitsu did not pull away. Progress, or shock. I would take either.
“No debt,” Marok said quietly.
Kitsu’s restored tail trembled behind him. “You keep saying that as if debt is the only danger.”
Marok went still. The Court House listened. Of course it did. Lies could not enter here. Evasions bled. Silence itself had begun leaning toward us with paperwork.
Marok looked at Kitsu. “Then what is?”

Kitsu’s jaw tightened. Gold light flickered at his throat. Not magic. Consequence. He could not lie. He could not flee, not without the Court House making a meal of his tongue. His gaze came to me, then away, then back because this place allowed no beautiful detours.
“Wanting,” he said.
The word was barely sound. The hall chimed. Truth. My heart behaved terribly.
Kitsu looked at the floor as if black marble might show him a more lawful version of himself. “I wanted her before I had permission to want anything.”
The confession landed in the Court House and became architecture. Every gold vein in the marble lit at once. Every witness chair stilled. Marok’s breath caught. Mine did not behave at all.

Kitsu continued because the truth had found its teeth and was no longer willing to sit quietly behind them. “I wanted her in the Celestial Court. Before the Mortal Hour. Before I could call it crisis or anchor or necessity. I wanted her when she counted exits and pretended not to. I wanted her when she looked at me as if I were a locked door and an insult. And I told myself law made wanting irrelevant.”
His mouth curved without humor. “That was a lie.”
The Court House rang once. Clean.
I should have stepped back. I did not.
Marok looked at me, then at Kitsu. His green tail burned low, uncertain for the first time since it had been restored. “So did I,” he said. No joke. No grin. Just the words.
The hall accepted them with a softer chime.

Marok swallowed. “I wanted her before I knew what the wanting cost. I wanted her when I thought wanting was only a better kind of trouble. And then I wanted her more when she made it clear she was not a prize for winning.” His gaze met mine. “I still do.”
Another chime. Truth.

My borrowed body chose that moment to remember breathing was an elective skill. My knees considered staging a protest. My mouth remembered both of them and became, once again, my least trustworthy feature.
The sensible thing would have been to retreat. I had been sensible for centuries. Overrated.
I stepped closer. Not into either of them. Between them. My place, because I chose it, not because Beauty arranged me there.
“Good,” I said.
Marok blinked. Kitsu looked pained. “Good?”
“Yes. It would be embarrassing if I had done all that kissing alone.”
Marok made a strangled sound that wanted badly to be laughter. Kitsu closed his eyes. The Court House, to its credit, did not chime. Apparently mortifying honesty was still honesty.
I lifted my hands, palm inward to each of them. “I am not fleeing. I am also not choosing a victor in the middle of a divine basement while a false commencement breathes down our necks.”
Marok’s mouth softened. “Reasonable.”
“Do not sound surprised.”
“I am full of surprises.”
“Most are structural hazards.”
Kitsu opened his eyes. The heat in them was steadier now. Not controlled away. Chosen down to a bearable flame. “What are you choosing?”
The question did not scrape. It was honest. Dangerous. Better.

I took both their hands. Rose light rose from my palms. Green answered. Gold followed. The braided thread wound around our joined fingers, not a chain, not a claim, but something that held because we did.
“I am choosing not to lie,” I said. “Not to myself. Not to either of you. Not in the only court ugly enough to deserve that much respect.”
The Court House chimed. Truth.

The Scale halves turned toward the dais. At the far end of the hall, beneath the three panless scales, a door appeared where no door had been. Blackened gold. Silver at the seams. Behind it came a sound like a bell tongue striking bone.
My missing tail answered. Magenta light leaked beneath the door. Not rose. Magenta. My physical color. My tail. My cage.
The air filled with crushed flowers after rain, then honey, smoke, and regret. Veyr’s silver scent threaded beneath it: extinguished candles, cheap violets, wet iron.
Marok’s hand tightened in mine, then eased at once. Kitsu’s did the same. They were learning. So was I.
The restored gold of Kitsu’s tail burned behind him. The green of Marok’s curled around his shadow. Between us, the braid held.
Beyond the door, someone laughed without sound. Veyr.
No foxes, the silver seam shaped.
The Scale halves sang together, still apart, still broken, still trying.
Kitsu looked at the door. “The Scales are beneath us.”
Marok looked at me. “And your tail.”
I smiled because Beauty had taught me how to make terror look deliberate, and tonight I had begun to enjoy using her lessons poorly.
“Then let us be rude,” I said.
The door opened. Beyond it, the undercourt waited with its blackened-gold teeth, a forged Bell-tongue glowing silver at its center, and my magenta tail burning in Veyr’s hands.
The Court House allowed no lies. Good. I had several truths sharpened and ready.
